name: inference
context: Extract TypeScript types from Zod schemas with z.infer, z.input, and z.output — single source of truth for runtime and compile time
info: Type inference is Zod's killer feature: define a schema once and extract the TypeScript type from it, so runtime validation and compile-time types are always in sync. z.infer<typeof schema> extracts the output type (after transforms). z.input<typeof schema> extracts the input type (before transforms). z.output<typeof schema> is identical to z.infer. When a schema has no transforms, input and output types are the same. This eliminates the classic problem of type definitions drifting from validation logic. Agents use this pattern constantly: define the Zod schema, infer the type, and use both throughout the codebase. See [[zod/schemas]] for defining shapes and [[zod/transform]] for when input/output types diverge.
triggers: single source of truth for types

sections.title: z.infer — extracting the output type
sections.body: `z.infer<typeof schema>` extracts the TypeScript output type from any Zod schema. This is the type of the value returned by `.parse()`. For schemas without transforms, input and output types are identical. Always use `typeof` with the schema variable: `z.infer<typeof MySchema>`, not `z.infer<MySchema>`. The convention is to define both the schema and the type with the same name: `const User = z.object({...})` and `type User = z.infer<typeof User>`.

sections.title: z.input and z.output — before and after transforms
sections.body: `z.input<typeof schema>` gives the type BEFORE transforms, defaults, and catches are applied — this is what you pass to `.parse()`. `z.output<typeof schema>` gives the type AFTER — identical to `z.infer`. When a schema has `.transform()`, `.default()`, or `.catch()`, input and output differ: a string field with `.transform(Number)` has input `string` and output `number`. Fields with `.default()` are optional in the input type but required in the output type. Use `z.input` when typing form state or raw API payloads, and `z.output`/`z.infer` for the processed result.

sections.title: Inference with generics and utility types
sections.body: Pass Zod schemas as generic parameters: `function validate<T extends z.ZodTypeAny>(schema: T, data: unknown): z.infer<T>`. The return type automatically infers from the schema argument. Key utility types: `z.ZodTypeAny` (base for all schemas), `z.ZodType<Output, Def, Input>` (parameterized), `z.ZodObject<Shape>` (object schemas), `z.ZodRawShape` (`{ [k: string]: z.ZodTypeAny }`). For recursive schemas, annotate explicitly with `z.ZodType<YourInterface>` since TypeScript cannot infer circular types.

sections.title: Schema-first design pattern
sections.body: The recommended Zod workflow: (1) define the schema as the single source of truth, (2) infer the type with `z.infer`, (3) use both schema and type throughout the codebase, (4) validate at system boundaries (API handlers, form submissions, env parsing). This eliminates type duplication — change the schema and the type updates automatically. The schema validates at runtime, the type validates at compile time, and they never drift apart.
